metadata.title: Why Your Students' CLAUDE.md Is the Best Evidence They Conducted the Build
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Teachers candidate 10. The mechanism: AI can generate the code but cannot generate the record of the human decisions that governed the code — the build log (specifications, plan-mode edits, handoff conditions, capacity labels, /rewind moments) is an assessment artifact that measures what AI cannot produce for the student, making it the best evidence of conducting discipline.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = build log / conducting discipline / human decisions; CRIMSON = code alone / AI-generated output / undifferentiated; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO academic integrity policy discussion, NO plagiarism detection comparison, NO build-log rubric scoring in full, NO peer-review methodology.

beats.narration_text: The teacher grades two student submissions. Both websites look identical. Both have clean code. One student conducted the build. One did not. The code cannot tell the teacher which is which.

card.copy: Why the build log is the assessment you cannot fake

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] build log is the assessment you cannot fake

beats.narration_text: Student A's build log: five-element specifications at each step. Plan-mode edits documented — what changed and why. Three handoff conditions, each binary and testable. Two supervisory-capacity labels with reasoning. One slash-rewind moment with the respecification. One Lessons Learned entry in CLAUDE.md. Student B's build log: a list of one-line prompts. No gates. No conditions. No labels.

production_viz.label: two build logs: one with conducting evidence, one with bare prompts
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'Student A' (teal) — rows: 'five-element spec', 'plan-mode edit documented', 'three handoff conditions', 'capacity labels', '/rewind + respec', 'Lessons Learned'; right 'Student B' (crimson) — rows: 'one-line prompt', 'one-line prompt', 'one-line prompt', '(no gates)', '(no conditions)', '(no labels)'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-column log comparison: conducting vs. bare prompts

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. If AI can generate working code, assessing the code output should measure AI capability rather than student capability. Here is the assessment artifact that measures what AI cannot produce for the student. Why?

card.copy: Same code. Different discipline. How do you tell?

beats.narration_text: AI can generate the code. It cannot generate the record of the human decisions made before and during execution. The five-element specification required the student to decide operation, invariants, context, output format, and negative constraint before any code ran. The plan-mode edit documented what the student changed in the plan and why. The handoff condition named what the student verified before proceeding. Those decisions cannot be cleanly generated by AI from the code alone.

production_viz.label: AI generates the code; AI cannot generate the decision record
production_viz.mechanic: two boxes: left 'what AI can generate' (crimson) — 'working HTML/CSS/JS'; right 'what AI cannot generate' (teal) — four rows: 'the specification written before the prompt', 'the plan-mode edit made by the student', 'the handoff condition that gated the step', 'the /rewind + respec that fixed the failure'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two boxes: AI generates code vs. AI cannot generate decision record

beats.narration_text: The build-log rubric. Five dimensions, each scored zero to four. Specification quality — are the five elements specific and load-bearing? Gate execution — is each step documented through explore, plan, implement, commit? Handoff conditions — are they testable and binary, with failures resolved? Capacity labeling — are at least three of the five capacities exercised? Lessons learned — does the log produce an entry suitable for CLAUDE.md?

production_viz.label: five-dimension rubric: each scored 0-4; total 20 points
production_viz.mechanic: five teal rows drawing in: '1. Specification quality — five elements specific and load-bearing'; '2. Gate execution — every step gated with plan edits'; '3. Handoff conditions — testable, binary, /rewind documented'; '4. Capacity labeling — at least three capacities labeled'; '5. Lessons learned — one entry suitable for CLAUDE.md'; a column on the right showing '0-4' for each

beats.narration_text: The score interpretation. Sixteen to twenty: conducting evident. The log shows a student who made decisions before Claude started and gated before Claude continued. Eleven to fifteen: conducting partial — some elements present, some missing. Six to ten: framework present but uneven. Zero to five: framework absent. The code grade and the build-log score can diverge substantially. That is the point.

production_viz.label: score: 16-20 conducting evident; 11-15 partial; 6-10 uneven; 0-5 absent
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal scale from 0 to 20: segment 0-5 in crimson 'absent'; 6-10 in amber 'uneven'; 11-15 in SLATE 'partial'; 16-20 in teal 'conducting evident'; Student A marker at 17, Student B marker at 7

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] score scale with two student markers

beats.narration_text: Here is where the CLAUDE.md comes in. The teacher's own CLAUDE.md — three builds in, three months of Lessons Learned — is the most pedagogically valuable artifact in the room. Not the textbook's example. The actual file. The students book describes the artifact. The teacher has the artifact. Showing students your CLAUDE.md, with the Lessons Learned entries and the dangerous-middle stories behind them, is the demonstration the framework cannot provide on its own.
beats.scene_description: a printed CLAUDE.md document spread open, showing multiple sections including a Lessons Learned section, editorial collage, desaturated, aged newsprint
beats.image_prompt: B07, a printed CLAUDE.md document spread open on aged newsprint, showing multiple sections of text including what appears to be a lessons learned section, the document looking well-used and annotated, editorial collage style, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones, no human faces, no readable text
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] CLAUDE.md document spread open with Lessons Learned section visible

beats.narration_text: The practical move: assess the build log, not only the code. For the first two builds, formative — the log shapes feedback and conversation. For the capstone, summative — the build log replaces or supplements the code grade. Peer review does most of the scoring against the rubric. The teacher's time goes to the disagreements and the high-impact follow-up. The economics favor this. The code grade alone measures AI capability. The build-log grade measures the student.

production_viz.label: assessment plan: formative first two builds, summative for capstone
production_viz.mechanic: three teal rows: 'Build 1: formative — shapes feedback, not graded'; 'Build 2: formative — rubric calibration via peer review'; 'Build 3 (capstone): summative — build log supplements or replaces code grade'; a note 'peer review handles scoring; teacher reviews disagreements'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three-build assessment plan: formative to summative

beats.narration_text: AI can generate the code. It cannot generate the build log. The build log is the assessment artifact that measures what is irreducibly human in a conducted build.

card.copy: Assess the build log. That is the conducting you cannot fake.
